title: Hormuz Standoff Escalates as Strikes Resume, Farnborough Opens Under Iran War Shadow, Helsing Raises $1.8B
subhead: US-Iran hostilities reignited after a collapsed ceasefire, with new strikes on Iranian coastal targets and Tehran threatening to shut all regional energy corridors. Meanwhile, Europe's defense tech boom hits a new peak as Helsing's $1.8B round becomes the continent's largest, and Farnborough prepares for a show dominated by alliance strain and munitions demand.

sections.heading: US-Iran Escalation: Strikes Resume, Hormuz Closure Deepens
sections.body:
The US conducted a new wave of strikes against Iran on Wednesday, targeting coastal defense systems and cruise missile storage sites on Greater Tunb Island, according to US Central Command. The strikes lasted roughly 90 minutes and followed a seven-hour bombardment of dozens of military targets near the Strait of Hormuz the day before, Reuters reports.

Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps claimed it struck US military targets in Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan in response, and threatened to shut down all regional energy export corridors beyond Hormuz, potentially including the Bab el-Mandeb strait via its Houthi allies. The IRGC said Hormuz would remain closed until 'the end of America's evils.'

Trump threatened to hit Iranian power plants and bridges next week unless Tehran resumes negotiations, while briefly floating and then scrapping a 20% fee on Hormuz shipping. An interim ceasefire deal signed last month was meant to lead to broader negotiations, but talks have faltered. Iran says at least 30 civilians and 7 military personnel were killed in recent strikes.
sections.whyItMatters: The collapse of even a limited ceasefire means the Hormuz disruption is structural, not temporary. Iran's threat to expand closures to Bab el-Mandeb would put two of the world's five most critical energy chokepoints at risk simultaneously.

sections.heading: Farnborough Air Show Opens Amid Alliance Strain and Spending Debates
sections.body:
The Farnborough International Air Show runs July 21-24 under a cloud of transatlantic tension. The preceding RIAT military airshow was canceled due to the Iran war, and Farnborough will absorb its presence — with record US company attendance, according to Aerospace Industries Association CEO Eric Fanning.

Fanning said the environment is more charged than any previous show: 'There's so much focus on, pressure on, and questions about the transatlantic alliance and the future of the alliance.' The show follows a bumpy NATO summit and Trump's continued push for European allies to spend more — while also pressing them to buy American arms, a policy analysts at Defense One describe as 'contradictory.'

Teal Group analyst J.J. Gertler said the key question is what European indigenous programs emerge after a year of scrambling to fill the US reliability gap. The UK unveiled a $298 billion Defense Investment Plan targeting space, autonomous systems, and cyber, committing to 3% GDP defense spending in the next Parliament and 3.5% by 2035. Meanwhile, the Franco-German FCAS fighter program collapsed last month, with both nations now exploring separate paths.

On the industry side, BAE Systems is unveiling a next-gen fighter pilot helmet, Northrop Grumman is pushing F-16 electronic warfare upgrades, and Raytheon is showcasing its Coyote counter-drone system in its first major show since combat deployment in multiple theaters.
sections.whyItMatters: Farnborough is the first major defense expo since the Iran war began and since the NATO summit exposed deep alliance fractures. What gets ordered and announced here will shape European procurement trajectories for years.

sections.heading: Helsing Raises $1.8B in Europe's Largest Defense-Tech Round
sections.body:
German AI defense company Helsing raised $1.8 billion in a Series E round, valuing the firm at $18 billion — the largest funding round ever for a European defense-tech startup. Investor demand 'significantly' exceeded available allocation, the company said in a statement.

Founded only in 2021, Helsing's valuation now rivals traditional European defense primes. The company offers the HX-2 strike drone, Altra AI battlefield operations platform, and the proposed CA-1 autonomous fighter jet concept, and partners with Rheinmetall, Kongsberg, and Saab on AI integration. Existing investors include Prima Materia, Accel, and Greenoaks, with Lightspeed Venture Partners and General Catalyst joining in this round.

The round underscores a cascade of European defense-tech mega-rounds: Quantum Systems raised $1.2B (valued at $8B) earlier this month, Stark raised €500M (valued at €3.2B) in June, ICEYE raised €450M in a Series F, Kraken Technology raised $175M (valued at $1B), and France's Harmattan AI raised $200M in January to become the country's first defense unicorn, Defense News reports.
sections.whyItMatters: European defense tech is no longer a niche bet — venture capital is treating it like a scale play. Helsing's $18B valuation in five years signals that AI-driven defense platforms are becoming the new primes, not supplements to them.

sections.heading: Navy Awards $2.2B for Medium Landing Ships, RIMPAC Tests Autonomous Supply
sections.body:
The US Navy awarded TOTE Services a $2.2 billion Vessel Construction Management contract for its new Medium Landing Ship program, with Bollinger Shipyards building one LSM and Fincantieri Marinette Marine building four. Construction starts this year, with first delivery in fall 2029.

The LSMs — measuring roughly 100 meters and designed for island-hopping logistics in the Indo-Pacific — are part of Trump's 'Golden Fleet' initiative. The ships can beach themselves to deliver over 800 tons of cargo with a range of 3,400 nautical miles, based on Damen Naval's proven LST-100 design already in service with Nigeria and selected by Australia.

Meanwhile, at RIMPAC 2026, Rear Adm. Michael Mattis called the exercise's advanced manufacturing demonstration the US military's largest yet. The effort coordinated by Fleetwerx and the Naval Postgraduate School tested autonomous boats and 3D printers to solve Pacific logistics. Splash Industries' Typhoon USV executed the first autonomous resupply of a warship at sea, delivering cargo to the USS Essex via well deck, then navigating 100 miles of open ocean to resupply an aircraft carrier. Firestorm demonstrated expeditionary manufacturing cells, Fieldmade showed a parachute-deployable 3D printer, and Havoc exhibited a 14-foot autonomous boat it printed for the exercise.
sections.whyItMatters: The Navy is investing billions in distributed logistics for the Pacific, but the gap between prototype demos and operational fleet integration remains wide. RIMPAC's manufacturing experiments suggest the Pentagon is serious about solving the 'tyranny of distance' — but 2029 delivery for the first LSM shows how long institutional shipbuilding still takes.

sections.heading: 155mm Ammo Production Stuck at 36% of Target, IG Report Reveals
sections.body:
Despite a congressional mandate to produce 100,000 155mm artillery rounds per month by October 2025, the Army has only reached 36,000 rounds per month as of March 2026, according to a Department of Defense Inspector General report published this month.

The bottleneck is projectile metal part production. The $469 million Universal Artillery Projectile Lines facility in Mesquite, Texas — operated by General Dynamics Ordnance and Tactical Systems — has been unable to produce any metal parts that meet contract specifications. The Army accepted the risk of adapting M107-era (1958) production equipment to make newer M795 projectile parts, a gamble the IG report says has not paid off.

Army officials also raised concerns that the Mesquite contract was not opened to competition and that the older Scranton, Pennsylvania plant had ongoing problems with contractor responsiveness, equipment maintenance, and timeliness.

Production has still nearly tripled from 14,000 to 36,000 rounds per month. The Army now hopes to reach 140,000 rounds per month by December 2027 by modernizing the Iowa Army Ammunition Plant and opening two new facilities in Kansas and Arkansas.
sections.whyItMatters: Two years after Congress mandated the ramp-up, the US still cannot produce more than a third of its 155mm shell target. The Mesquite failure is a case study in how not to scale defense production — adapting unproven Cold War equipment instead of building from scratch.

sections.heading: Hydis Interceptor Concept Validated, Germany Picks Saab for New Frigates
sections.body:
Europe's Hydis hypersonic interceptor program completed its final concept review milestone, settling on a solid-propellant rocket motor architecture, MBDA announced. The program — backed by France, Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands through OCCAR — advances to technology maturation at Technology Readiness Level 3, with €80M in EDF funding and €60M from partner nations.

The interceptor is designed to defeat hypersonic glide vehicles, hypersonic cruise missiles, and maneuvering ballistic missiles. Key contributors include Avio (solid rocket motor), ArianeGroup/Roxel (kill vehicle control), Lynred (infrared sensor), and Thales (naval/ground sensor suites). MBDA received over €60M across seven entities. Hydis competes with the Hydef program led by Germany's Diehl Defence (€100M EDF funding), with only one to be selected for full development under the EDF 2026 work program allocating €114M for an endo-atmospheric interceptor.

Separately, Saab won a $900 million (8.7B kronor) contract to supply combat systems and sensors for Germany's four new MEKO A-200 DEU frigates. The deal with prime contractor TKMS comes after Berlin scrapped the troubled F126 frigate program last month, pivoting to an off-the-shelf design for urgent NATO anti-submarine warfare requirements. The Bundestag approved the €6.3B ($7.2B) procurement on July 8, with an option for four additional hulls. Saab will deliver its 9LV combat management system, Sea Giraffe 4A Fixed Face radar, and Sea Giraffe 1X lightweight radar between 2029 and 2032.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe is investing in both layered missile defense and rapid naval recapitalization. The Hydis/Hydef competition will determine whether Europe builds its own hypersonic shield or remains dependent on US systems. Germany's pivot from the bespoke F126 to off-the-shelf MEKO frigates with Saab systems signals that speed now trumps sovereignty in European naval procurement.

sections.heading: China Coast Guard Patrols East of Taiwan, SLBM Test Signals Nuclear Triad
sections.body:
China's Coast Guard has maintained sustained patrols 54 nautical miles east of Taiwan since May, rotating two vessel formations in what Beijing describes as 'routine law enforcement.' Analysts say the deployment represents a qualitative shift from short naval drills to persistent presence, exploiting the Coast Guard's longer endurance to establish a new normal.

Taiwan's Mainland Affairs Council called the patrols a violation of international law. Joshua Arostegui of the US Army War College noted that large coast guard ships are designed for extended deployments — 'that's the big takeaway.' Sophie Wushuang Yi of Tsinghua University said the rotation pattern and stated intent all point toward making this 'routine.'

The patrols responded to maritime boundary negotiations between Japan and the Philippines, both near Taiwan, according to analysts. China condemned the talks, signaling that it views bilateral maritime agreements among neighbors as challenges to its claims.

Separately, China's July 6 submarine-launched ballistic missile test — its first into international waters — covered 7,300 kilometers with a simulated training warhead, likely overflying parts of the Philippines, according to CSIS. The test coincided with Australia signing a defense treaty with Fiji. Analysts said the launch demonstrated China's nuclear triad capability and was designed to warn regional powers against hosting foreign military infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: China is layering grey-zone persistence with strategic deterrence. The coast guard patrols east of Taiwan create a new baseline of presence that will be hard to reverse, while the SLBM test signals that the nuclear dimension of Indo-Pacific competition is maturing — not just for China's domestic audience, but to discourage regional basing arrangements.

bottomLine: The Iran-US conflict reignited after a collapsed ceasefire, with strikes on Iranian coastal sites and Tehran threatening to expand energy corridor closures beyond Hormuz. Farnborough opens next week under the longest shadow of alliance uncertainty in its modern history. Helsing's $1.8B round confirms that European defense tech has become a mainstream institutional investment category, not a venture bet. The US Army's 155mm ammunition production remains stuck at 36% of its mandated target two years after Congress ordered the ramp-up. Germany's pivot to off-the-shelf MEKO frigates with Saab systems and Europe's Hydis hypersonic interceptor milestone both signal that speed and proven designs are winning over bespoke national programs. China's coast guard persistence east of Taiwan and its SLBM demonstration are two edges of the same strategy: creating facts on the ground and facts in the sky.
sources: Defense News, Defense One, Reuters, CSIS, OCCAR, MBDA, Saab, US Navy, US Army, DoD Inspector General, TOTE Services, Fleetwerx, Breaking Defense, Al Jazeera, White House, US Central Command
